prompt: Where in the corpus does an author pair a remark of Sully to Henri IV — that to subject one's subjects by force of arms one must keep one's 'backside always in the saddle, a hauberk on your back, a helmet on your head, a pistol in your fist, and a sword in your hand' — with a boast attributed to Bonaparte that 'I will go to Madrid, I will go to Vienna with an army of five hundred thousand men,' before observing that with such an army 'one can go to die, captive and forsaken, on a desert rock in the middle of the Atlantic'? Quote the passage and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The juxtaposition of Sully's hauberk-and-helmet line with Bonaparte's 500,000-men boast and the Saint-Helena allusion is unique to this work; no other corpus title (the Comte volumes, Iqbal, Fichte, the Old English / Sanskrit / Old Javanese material, or the Urdu diwans) pairs these two figures or makes this argument that domination ruins one's own liberty.

prompt: In the corpus, where is the dynamometer cited as evidence against Rousseau's claim that the savage is physically stronger than the civilized man — with a numerical 'progression' of 50, 51, 58, 69 representing, in order, the natives of Diemen's Land, of New Holland, of Timor, and the French sailors who had just disembarked from a long voyage? Quote the passage that gives this progression, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The exact numerical progression 50/51/58/69 attached to Diemen's Land, New Holland, Timor, and the French (drawn from Péron's voyage to the southern lands) is a unique, verbatim-quotable factoid present only in this work; it could not be retrieved from the Comte volumes or any other corpus item.

prompt: Which work in the corpus reconstructs the etymology of the word 'industry' from the Latin INDU (for INTUS, 'within') joined to STRUO ('I arrange, I set up, I construct'), and uses this philology to argue that the term was first applied to the cunning of swindlers — so that 'knights of industry' originally named men distinguished in fraud — citing the Abbé Galiani's claim, 'formally' written in 1750, that no fortune could grow without other fortunes being diminished? Quote the passage that gives the INTUS-STRUO derivation and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The combination of the INDU/INTUS-STRUO etymology, the 'knights of industry' (chevaliers d'industrie) gloss, and the dated 1750 reference to Abbé Galiani is a distinctive philological-economic argument unique to this work; no other corpus item (including the Comte legislation/property volumes) reconstructs the word 'industry' in this way.
